items.question: Biggest challenge/co-op achievement

responses: Feeling like you actually know what you’re doing
Implementing correct action on hardware that shipped with product
Onboarded a new data pipeline for conducting critical cell tests at Tesla
I took majority marketshare from a 100 person company
At my 3rd coop, I was a bozo but put in the hours to get the work done. It was the most foundational experience in my career and set me up for later co-ops and full time roles.
Deployed and developed an entire service on my own
Being the Lead Architect as a co-op
Dealing with a Non-Technical boss who didn't know what the company has or understand what is needed
Waking up at 4AM to watch the launch of the rocket you helped build with code on it that you wrote. Cheering with everyone was electric.
Getting my first proper software developer internship at Playstation after grinding hard to become good at JavaScript.
All zipline drones are currently kept legal (FAA regulations) by about 100 lines of my code.
Made a PCB that’s on a satellite in space
I technically got my full time offer during my second co op (third work term)
Learning how to survey using GNSS equipment worth thousands of dollars.
Worked on 2 million dollar 800G radio testers
Living alone in a small city with no car and no one I knew. It was a tough 4 months
Managing to get co-ops in the first place (I was unemployed first term).
Imposter syndrome, figuring out what I actually want to do outside of the Waterloo grindset influence
Plugging in and inspecting >1000 optical fibers
Resolving major performance bug inside CMS application at TVO
Interview getting and also having different types of roles
Still finding my first coop job when COVID first hit despite the chaos and fear everyone shared

items.question: Any fun interview stories?

responses: I slept through my first interview ever
I waited in front of a professor's door for 2 hours so I can chat with him and got a job offer from it
Asked why my marks were so low
An interviewer asked me “How would you test a watermelon?” Another interviewer asked me “Are you smart?”
The power to CMH went out during one of my interviews. Didn't get the job :(

Interviewed for a small tech company in SF. I was on a call with the CEO of the company, and it wasn't through WaterlooWorks so I let him know that the deadline was very soon and if he could get back to me within the next week that would be great. I get an email a couple days later telling me they're sorry, they've decided to go in a different direction on the internship, but could I please send them my address because they have some things they want to send me. I then get a package with like $300 worth of computer parts and some branded merch
Taught my interviewer python (this was a python coding interview)
One of my interviews had his daughter in the background showcasing and taking photos of her feet.
I've had interviewers not show up, or show up late and joined the interview from their car.

interviewer left during the interview lol

0 technical questions, talked about boats for 30 minutes. Got the job.

items.question: Do you have any coop-specific advice to share? Would you do anything differently?

responses: Always apply big, apply for the dream jobs whether through WW or externally, you never know when you'll hit.
No one else actually knows what they’re doing either
Start early and find a niche that you are into early. Don't just follow the crowd.
Use one of my co-ops to do something different than embedded software
Don't stress too much about early co-ops, upper-year glowup is real. If you focus your interests/projects in one area, you'll have an easier time standing out.
Do not return to the same employer unless the role provides significant learning experiences.
Start doing projects/learning actual software engineering (not just coding) early.
Don't stress too much about it, and take the opportunity to apply for weird shit and things you're super unqualified for just for the fun of it and the experience.
Pick a niche that you can beat 99.9% of everyone else in
I would apply to more external jobs and try to travel more
Coop is what you make it out to be. Even if you arent satisfied with your role, reach out, and you'd be surprised what you can dom

If you want to do hardware, don't join the crowds and become a code monkey like everyone else in first year. Join a design team and get experience in the field of your choice. The barrier to entry for good companies is lower than one might think.

Confidence is key, don't be afraid to ask for help and make sure you convince the interviewers that you're the best person for the job As for doing stuff differently, not really. Maybe if I grinded I could've gotten some US internships but I'm kind of a lazy bum so yea
Maybe try a few new places.
Worry less about not doing side projects/co-ops in my free time - I used to feel really guilty. Instead open source contribution and club related achievements go a lot further.

Being likable is just as important as being talented or hardworking. If you just go into work, head down, grinding, you're not gonna get as far as the person who others want to help and want to see succeed.
Grind leetcode ASAP. Building cool stuff like personal projects/design teams stand out. Grades matter! Good grades are a good quantifiable signal of your technical skills and hard work.
Find a field to specialize in. Some fields are easier to get into than others, but there are challenging problems in every field, so expertise is always in demand.
Don’t use WW. Also, cold outreach to startups is the easiest way to find the first few internships
Specialize in a field, it pays dividends to have domain expertise
Apply to everything. Like seriously, even if you aren't a good fit for what they interviewed you for, if you did a good job they may redirect you to a different team.
Always apply externally, keep looking for a better job, money is king.
My biggest regret is not reneging and going for a better company; I was scared of the consequences.
Join design teams. I regret not doing the same seeing where it took people for co-ops and full time positions
Apply to as many jobs as possible regardless of whether you actually meet the requirements (you're going to learn them on the job anyway).
If I did it again I would apply more externally instead of relying on WW
Your worth as a human isn't based on how much money you can make! Big tech shouldn't be your goal just because it's some people's goal
Try something money making for first coop job instead of doing full stack or data engineering
Take it more seriously. Still worked out well.

the amount of work and the justifiable complaining about the entire academic experience

My closest friends were made through trauma bonding.

learn to find the light in the tunnel - bad things always come with good things

Good routine is more important than studying hard

items.question: What's one piece of advice you’d give your first-year self?

responses: Unless you’re insane, your first co-ops are gonna suck
You are not alone, and a lot of people are going through similar struggles.
Take it easy. You’re young. Enjoy the next 5 years - you have the rest of your life to get stressed
Lock in early and build a good foundation of discipline
Try things to their max before deciding whether you like it or not, then ditch the stuff you don't like quickly.
Learn to Integrate and don't forget how to
Have confidence, you were accepted for a reason.
Don't follow others, they are usually wrong
Take some time to consider how important grades are vs personal enjoyment
Invest in bitcoin. More seriously though, learn to study more efficiently earlier on.
learn to not be an anti-social recluse earlier
It really gets better in fourth year

Worry/Plan less - money isn't that important. I didn't do 95% of the things I felt paranoid about I should be doing, and honestly I believe I am in the best possible situation my personal circumstances would allow me to be in. Even if I did all that I felt I should have been, I would have made slightly more money in the best case scenario

When you believe in your ability to do something, you’ll naturally put in the work to achieve it. But if you don’t believe in yourself, you’ll give up, thinking you can’t do things you’re actually capable of.
Enjoy the time, you're definitely going to miss it when its over.
Co-ops are meant to guide your future career, use them to find your technical interests.
perseverance - keep swimming and you will eventually reach the goal (dont feel rushed)
Don't Give up and study early
Pay attention in all your courses. You never know when you'll need to know something
Give less shit about how other people see you and just yap
Focus more on getting better co-ops
Time management and do an extra-curricular that isn’t stem related
Online uni is still a uni.
Work hard, apply to jobs early, bet on yourself
You have to put in more effort. Just because you made it to a big name school doesn't mean everything is guaranteed after that.
It’s okay to fail. It doesn’t matter how long it takes for you to finish ur degree. No one cares how long u took once u graduate
Seek mental health help earlier (therapy + antidepressants)
Be a bit more outgoing, msg first
Explore outside of your friend group’s activities
Network more and foster more relationships
It always works out in the end
Don’t worry about software, stick to hardware
Chatgpt will save your life, don’t stress too hard
don't panic, everything will work out
You'll pass those hard useless courses
learn software development (C or fullstack) on your own, because this degree does not teach you how to code
Enjoy the grind, have fun it'll be fine
Go to class if you’re slow learner. No point comparing yourself to others because they have different ways of doing stuff. Trust yourself and push yourself with your own skills.
It gets better and you make friends :)
Try everything, don’t try to specialize till after
difficulties can be overcome always, so just tell yourself it will be over soon (unless you die, which 99.99% will not happen)
Try harder (I failed courses, which is okay, but I really could have just not)
Stop slacking off for the first month every term
Take more time to relax and do things other than school or coop.
